summary: The post introduces the concept of a 'Request Stream Map' as a technique for exposing unnecessary complexity in enterprise systems, particularly ESBs. Erik Dörnenburg used this visualization approach to demonstrate how a simple request flows through multiple application layers, printing out pages of code and creating a diagram that made the overhead visible. The technique is analogous to Value Stream Mapping in agile, but applied to technical architecture. The visualization helped convince stakeholders to remove an unnecessary ESB from a system by making its cost apparent.
key_points: ESBs are often unnecessary complexity that teams should work to remove from systems
A Request Stream Map traces how data for a request/response moves through application layers
Visualizing the full code path helps identify excessive layering and architectural waste
Layering is essential but excess layering can be as problematic as too little
When transformations are necessary, prefer multiple small transformations over one large one
money_quotes: Hang around my colleagues at Thoughtworks and you soon get the impression that the only good Enterprise Service Bus (ESB) is a dead ESB.
Jim Webber refers to them as Erroneous Spaghetti Boxes.
Erik printed out all the code that you needed to read to understand how this would work - which ran to several pages.
Layering is an essential tool for building software applications. But like most essential things in life, excess can be almost as much of a problem as too little.
A visualization like this (or the multiple pages of code) can help you find where 'just enough' is.
If you do need to transform data from one form to another, it's usually better to do a few little transformations than one big transformation.
key_insight: Visualizing the full path of a request through your system layers exposes unnecessary complexity and helps you find the 'just enough' level of architecture.
